mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M276 V6 typically costs £3,500–£6,000 supply only, with the M278 twin‑turbo V8 higher at £4,500–£7,500. The M256 inline‑six from the C257 costs £5,000–£8,000. Fitting adds £1,300–£1,900. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your CLS.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends which engine your CLS has. The M276 (CLS350, C218/X218) is generally reliable but has a well‑documented timing chain tensioner weakness — listen for cold‑start rattle and check the engine number. The M278 (CLS500, C218/X218) delivers 408 hp but has more serious issues — cylinder scoring, oil leaks into the wiring harness, and timing chain wear. The M256 (C257) is the most modern and refined. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: M276 (CLS350): timing chain tensioner rattle (cold start), camshaft adjuster wear. M278 (CLS500): cylinder scoring (piston slap), oil leaks from camshaft sensors into the wiring harness, timing cover oil leaks, timing chain wear, and coolant loss. M256 (C257): early 48V battery and cooling system issues. Both V6 and V8 engines demand regular maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. A C218 CLS350 is a £8,000–£18,000 vehicle — a £4,000–£4,500 recon is 22–56% of the car's value. A C218 CLS500 is a £10,000–£22,000 vehicle — a £5,000–£6,000 recon is 23–60% of the car's value. A C257 CLS is a £20,000–£45,000 vehicle — a £6,000–£7,000 recon is 13–35% of the car's value. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation and condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The CLS350 covers M276 (C218/X218, 2011–2018). The CLS500 covers M278 (C218/X218, 2011–2018). The C257 (2018–2023) uses the M256. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: On M276: check the engine number — if within the affected range (up to 276 9xx 30 365996), install check valves and updated tensioners. On M278: regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved oil are essential to prevent cylinder scoring and timing chain wear. Check for oil leaks from camshaft sensors — address them promptly to prevent wiring harness and ECU damage. On both engines: use the 'severe schedule' (5,000 miles) for oil changes. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, piston slap or oil leaks, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the range across all generations; specific costs vary by era — see the Era Map above. M278 engines are significantly more expensive to replace than M276 units due to their complexity. A full engine replacement for a CLS500 can cost in the region of £7,000–£12,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a CLS350 / CLS500 petrol engine typically takes 14–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). V8 engines require more disassembly than V6 units.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Rattle (M276 — C218/X218 CLS350)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start (2–3 seconds), rattling or clattering from the front of the engine, poor timing performance, misfires.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,500 (tensioners, check valves and camshaft adjusters)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M276's timing chain tensioners are the most common failure point. They rely on oil pressure to maintain tension; if they drain down when the engine is stood, the chains will rattle on startup. Mercedes issued a technical service bulletin (LI05.10‑P‑056435) — engines up to number 276 9xx 30 365996 require both check valves and new tensioners; later engines up to 406603 require check valves only. If you hear a cold‑start rattle, have the tensioners and check valves inspected immediately.

problems.title: Cylinder Scoring & Timing Chain Wear (M278 — C218/X218 CLS500)
problems.symptoms: Piston slap on startup, loss of power, increased oil consumption, metallic knocking, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £4,000–£8,000 (engine rebuild or cylinder bore remediation)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,500–£9,500 (full engine replacement if scoring is extensive)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear piston slap or knocking, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M278 twin‑turbo V8 has several serious reported issues. Cylinder scoring (deep scratches in the cylinder walls) has been documented in multiple teardowns. Oil leaks from camshaft position sensors can seep into the wiring harness and potentially damage the ECU. Timing chain wear and timing cover oil leaks are also common. If buying a CLS500, listen carefully for piston slap on startup and consider having the cylinders bore‑scoped. A full service history with regular oil changes is essential.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Camshaft Sensors & Timing Covers (M278 — C218/X218 CLS500)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, oil residue on the engine, oil leaking into the wiring harness, electrical gremlins.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,500 (camshaft sensor seals, timing cover reseal)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil leaks into the wiring harness

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are a prevalent issue on the M278. Camshaft position sensors can leak oil into the wiring harness, potentially reaching the ECU. The upper front timing covers and valve covers are also common leak points — Mercedes uses machined mating surfaces with sealant instead of traditional rubber gaskets, and this sealant degrades over time. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the engine inspected immediately — addressing oil leaks promptly prevents expensive wiring harness and ECU damage.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Install check valves and updated tensioners — permanent fix
Often requires full rebuild; assess overall engine health

rows.problem: Oil leaks — cam sensors & timing covers (M278)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Address promptly — prevents wiring harness and ECU damage
Catch it early — before chain jumps
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * M276 (CLS350): check valves and tensioners replaced (or engine outside the affected range)
* M278 (CLS500): no piston slap on cold start — cylinders bore‑scoped and clear
* No oil leaks — camshaft sensors and timing covers dry
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes
avoidIf: * M276: cold‑start rattle with no tensioner history — walk away
* M278: piston slap on startup — cylinder scoring likely — walk away
* M278: oil leaks with no diagnosis — potential wiring harness and ECU damage
* Poor service history — both engines are sensitive to neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The CLS350 and CLS500 petrol V6 and V8 engines represent the performance heart of the CLS range. The M276 is generally reliable but has a well‑documented timing chain tensioner weakness — check the engine number and listen for cold‑start rattle. The M278 twin‑turbo V8 delivers effortless power but has more serious issues — cylinder scoring, oil leaks into the wiring harness, and timing chain wear. The C257's M256 is the most modern and refined choice. Buy on full service history — and never ignore a cold‑start rattle or piston slap.

cta.label: → Compare CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling (M276 tensioners) or piston slap (M278 scoring)
* Engine Number (M276) — Check if within affected range (up to 276 9xx 30 365996 requires tensioners + check valves)
* Oil Leaks (M278) — Check camshaft sensors and timing covers; oil in wiring harness is a red flag
* Service History — including which generation (C218/X218 or C257) and confirmed oil‑change intervals
* Cylinder Scoring (M278) — Listen for piston slap; consider bore‑scoping
* Coolant Loss (M278) — Check for unexplained loss; head gasket or cooling system issues possible
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures

Fitting adds £1,300–£1,900. A full engine replacement for a CLS500 can cost £7,000–£12,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your CLS.

items.question: Is the Mercedes CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine reliable?
items.answer: It depends which engine your CLS has. The M276 (CLS350, C218/X218) is generally reliable but has a well‑documented timing chain tensioner weakness — listen for cold‑start rattle and check the engine number. The M278 (CLS500, C218/X218) delivers 408 hp but has more serious issues — cylinder scoring, oil leaks into the wiring harness (potentially damaging the ECU), and timing chain wear. The M256 (C257) is the most modern and refined. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

items.question: What are the common problems with the CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine?
items.answer: M276 (CLS350): timing chain tensioner rattle (cold start), camshaft adjuster wear. M278 (CLS500): cylinder scoring (piston slap), oil leaks from camshaft sensors into the wiring harness, timing cover oil leaks, timing chain wear, and coolant loss. M256 (C257): early 48V battery and cooling system issues. Both V6 and V8 engines demand regular maintenance.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on vehicle value and generation. A C218 CLS350 is a £8,000–£18,000 vehicle — a £4,000–£4,500 recon is 22–56% of the car's value. A C218 CLS500 is a £10,000–£22,000 vehicle — a £5,000–£6,000 recon is 23–60% of the car's value. For lower‑value examples, replacement may approach our 70% Mercedes scrap threshold. A C257 CLS is a £20,000–£45,000 vehicle — a £6,000–£7,000 recon is 13–35% of the car's value; replacement is viable. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation and condition.

items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The CLS350 covers M276 (C218/X218, 2011–2018). The CLS500 covers M278 (C218/X218, 2011–2018). The C257 (2018–2023) uses the M256. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

items.question: Can I prevent the common failures on a CLS350 / CLS500 Petrol?
items.answer: On M276: check the engine number — if within the affected range (up to 276 9xx 30 365996), install check valves and updated tensioners. On M278: regular oil changes with Mercedes‑approved oil are essential to prevent cylinder scoring and timing chain wear. Check for oil leaks from camshaft sensors — address them promptly to prevent wiring harness and ECU damage. Listen for piston slap on cold start and consider bore‑scoping the cylinders. On both engines: use the "severe schedule" (5,000 miles) for oil changes. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, piston slap or oil leaks, book an inspection immediately.
